text: From BBC Learning English.

This is Learning English from the News,
our podcast about the news headlines.
In this programme,

US seizes Venezuelan
President Maduro.
Hello, I'm Pippa.
And I'm Beth.

In this programme we look
at one big news story

and the vocabulary in the headlines
that will help you understand it.

You can find all the vocabulary
and headlines from this episode,

as well as a worksheet
on our website, bbclearningenglish.com.

So, Beth, let's hear more
about this story.

A US military operation
in the Venezuelan capital Caracas

on Saturday led to the arrest
of its president, Nicolas Maduro,
and his wife, Cilia Flores.

The couple were taken to New York
and appeared in court

on Monday accused of conspiring
with terrorist organisations.
'Conspire' means secretly plan.

They were also accused
of trafficking cocaine into the US.

Both Maduro
and Flores pleaded not guilty.

Following the military action
in Venezuela,

US President Donald Trump said
the US would 'run' Venezuela
for an unspecified amount of time.
Let's have our first headline.

This comes from Le Monde, a French
newspaper which also reports in English.

Maduro's fate lies with US courts,
as Venezuela's lies with Trump.
That headline, again from Le Monde.

Maduro's fate lies with US courts,
as Venezuela's lies with Trump.

This headline is about the charges
against Nicolas Maduro, as well as
the plan for the future of Venezuela.

Now we're interested in a phrase
that is repeated in the headline,
'fate lies with'. Pippa,
what can you tell us?
Well, let's break it down.

A person's fate is what will happen
to them in the future.

We often use 'fate' to talk
about dramatic things like winning

or losing, life relationships,
careers, or even death.

That's right. And if someone's fate
lies with someone or something,

it means their fate will be decided
by that person or thing.

Their fate, or future, depends
on the person or thing. Yes.

So, in the headline, Maduro's fate
lies with the US courts.

This means the US courts will decide
what happens to Nicolas Maduro.
His future depends on the courts.

Right. And the headline also says
Venezuela's fate lies with Trump.

This means what happens to Venezuela
in the future depends
on the actions of President Trump.

We see this phrase used in
dramatic news stories about change,

where what will happen in the future
is uncertain, and it's quite poetic.

For example,
if a country holds an election,

you could say the fate
of the government lies with voters.

Yes. Or another example, the fate
of the company lies with its CEO.
We've had 'fate
lies with', future depends on.

For example, the fate of the new
railway lies with town planners.
This is Learning English

from the News, our podcast
about the news headlines.

Today, we're talking
about the US's operation to seize
Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro.

Following the arrest
of Nicolas Maduro.

His deputy leader Delcy Rodriguez has
become interim president in Venezuela.

At an emergency meeting
of the UN Security Council,

many countries criticised the actions
of the US,
saying they broke international law.

But many countries agreed with the US
that Nicolas Maduro had been

an illegitimate leader,
meaning that Maduro did not win

a free and fair election
to become president of Venezuela.
Let's have another headline.
This one's from BBC News.

Trump's seizure of Maduro raises
thorny legal questions.

And that headline
again from BBC News.

Trump's seizure of Maduro
raises thorny legal questions.

This headline is from an article
about whether the US military actions

in Venezuela, and the arrest of
Nicolas Maduro, broke international law.

The headline says Trump's decision
raises thorny legal questions.

We're looking
at this adjective 'thorny'.

So literally, thorns are sharp spikes
that are found on some plants.

For example,
in the UK, roses are thorny.

But 'thorny' can also be used
metaphorically to mean difficult
or complicated.

Yes, imagine walking
through a thorny plant or bush.

You might get scratched, your clothes
might get caught on the thorns.
It would be difficult.

So, if a question is thorny,
it's difficult
and not simple to answer or solve.

We can use 'thorny' to describe questions,
problems, and issues in English.

For example, teachers are struggling
to deal with the thorny issue
of mobile phones in schools.

We've had 'thorny',
difficult or complicated.

For example, what to do about taxes
is a particularly thorny problem
for the government.

This is Learning English from
the News from BBC Learning English.

We're talking about
President Trump's decision to arrest
Venezuelan leader Nicolas Maduro.
After the US action to seize Maduro.

President Trump commented on other
countries where the US could intervene,

that means get involved
in, such as Colombia, Mexico and Iran.

Trump has also talked again
about wanting to control Greenland,

which he says is necessary
for US security.

Greenland's prime minister,
Jens-Frederik Nielsen,
called for a respectful dialogue,

and European leaders have said
that Greenland belongs to its people.
And our next headline is about this.
It comes from CNN.

Trump's Venezuela raid
plunges Greenland

and the Western military alliance
into uncertainty.
That headline again from CNN.

Trump's Venezuela raid
plunges Greenland

and the Western military alliance
into uncertainty.

So, this headline is about what the US
military action in Venezuela could mean
for Greenland and other countries,

the Western military alliance, which
means places that usually work together

with the US,
such as the European Union.

We're interested in the phrase 'plunge
into uncertainty'.

'Plunge' makes me think
of diving into water, Beth.

Yes, well,
that is the literal meaning.

'Plunge' as a verb means to move
suddenly forwards or downwards,
often into something like water.

'Plunge' can also mean to cause
someone to move suddenly,

and that's useful
for the meaning in the headline.

Yes, the headline says that Trump's
action in Venezuela plunges Greenland
into uncertainty.

It's metaphorical and means Trump's
actions have caused a sudden sense

of uncertainty or worry for Greenland
and for other US allies.

We commonly see 'plunge' used
with uncertainty.

You can also plunge someone into
other negative situations or feelings
like despair.
And another common use of 'plunge':
a place can be plunged into darkness.

That means
all the lights suddenly go out.

We've had 'plunge
into uncertainty', to cause

someone to suddenly feel uncertain
or worried.
For example,

when the venue cancelled
their wedding,

the couple were plunged
into uncertainty.

That's it for this episode
of Learning English from the News.

We'll be back next week
with another news story.

If you've enjoyed this programme, try
our Learning English Grammar podcast.

You can find all episodes
at bbclearningenglish.com.
And follow us on social media.
Search for BBC Learning English.

Goodbye for
now. Bye.
